safety-debt.body: Safety debt is one of the few phrases that improves when finance people hear it. Once the debt has a cost curve, the romance of shipping around it tends to fade.
safety-debt.example: "They called it a temporary launch exception until the safety debt started charging interest in the form of weekly firefights and increasingly theatrical approvals."
safety-debt.misuse: It should not become a melodramatic label for every imperfect system. Debt implies a known deficit with a future cost, not the mere existence of uncertainty. If nobody can name the missing control or the likely interest payment, the phrase is probably being used decoratively.
safety-debt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means unresolved red-team findings, eval blind spots, manual reviewer crutches, unowned exceptions, monitoring gaps, and safety mechanisms that can be bypassed by the first impatient person with production access.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The missing safeguards have not disappeared; they have merely been rescheduled to a more embarrassing moment.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Known control gaps that will make a future launch, audit, or incident materially worse.

safety-debt.whyExists: Teams rarely skip safety work because they hate safety. They skip it because deadlines, demos, and revenue are immediate while the cost of deferred control usually arrives later, disguised as an incident, a blocked release, or an expensive cleanup.

sam-altman.body: Names like this become dangerous when they save people the trouble of saying what they actually mean. If the room says "Sam", make it unpack the noun.

sandbox.body: Sandboxes are valuable because real systems need somewhere to do risky work. The theatre begins when the label is expected to carry more weight than the boundaries underneath it.
sandbox.example: "The code execution feature became tolerable once it ran in a sandbox instead of directly on the machine everyone would later pretend was important."
sandbox.misuse: The term gets treated as if any slightly inconvenient environment counts as safe. A sandbox reduces blast radius; it does not abolish it. Calling something sandboxed can become a tidy way of avoiding a more tedious conversation about escape routes, data access, or operator error.
sandbox.practicalMeaning: In practice it means allowlists, disposable state, time limits, network rules, mount points, audit logs, and clear answers about who can widen access when the task starts making demands. The real question is not whether a sandbox exists, but what it still permits.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: A constrained environment designed to make mistakes smaller and escalation harder.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

sandbox.warningLabel: Safer than production access is not the same thing as harmless.
sandbox.whyExists: It exists because useful systems often need to run code, inspect files, or call tools, and "trust us" remains a poor security architecture. Isolation creates room for action without granting production-level freedom by accident.

slop.body: The important distinction is not human versus machine. It is care versus convenience. AI simply makes the convenience side industrial.
slop.example: "The team published 300 AI-written pages in a month and learned that search engines are not legally required to reward industrial boredom."
slop.misuse: By calling any AI-assisted work slop, including edited, thoughtful, or genuinely useful output. Slop is not "made with AI". Slop is "made without care".
slop.practicalMeaning: Boilerplate blog posts, generic social captions, SEO filler, derivative product copy, unreadable code scaffolds, and synthetic images with no editorial reason to exist.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The system made something quickly, cheaply, and with so little care that the output became its own argument against scale.

slop.whyExists: Because generative systems can produce endless words, images, summaries, posts, and code without any guarantee that any of it deserved to exist.

slop-flood.body: The flood metaphor matters because it changes the unit of concern. A single weak artefact is an editorial problem. Industrial abundance is a systems problem.
slop-flood.example: "The model did not ruin the internet by writing one bad paragraph. It ruined it by making an ocean of nearly acceptable ones."
slop-flood.misuse: By talking as if volume alone is the issue. The real problem is not just scale. It is the collapse of meaningful filtering.
slop-flood.practicalMeaning: Search results worsen, feeds get repetitive, marketplaces fill with derivative junk, and the burden shifts to ranking systems, curators, and trusted brands.

slop-flood.whyExists: Because one bad article is annoying. Ten million are infrastructure.

askNext: Was this dependency verified against a real project, maintainer, and source repository?

What controls catch a convincing package name that should never have existed?
slopsquatting.body: It is a tidy example of the modern software stack: one system invents the dependency, another system publishes the trap, and the human appears mainly to confirm the transaction.
slopsquatting.example: "The prototype moved fast until someone realised the package only existed because the model had dreamt it and an attacker had been paying attention."
slopsquatting.misuse: The term can get stretched to mean any bad dependency advice from an AI tool. The more precise problem is the attacker exploiting hallucinated package names by occupying them before a human notices the package never legitimately existed.
slopsquatting.practicalMeaning: In practice it means verifying packages before installation, preferring known libraries over plausible ones, locking dependencies properly, and treating AI-suggested imports as a security surface rather than a convenience feature.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker monetising the gap between a model's plausible fiction and a developer's missing verification step.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Malicious packages published to catch AI-generated dependency hallucinations before a human notices.

slopsquatting.whyExists: It exists because code models generate plausible nonsense, package registries reward being first with a name, and some teams now review dependency suggestions with less suspicion than a spam folder.

askNext: What task quality is actually required, and is a smaller model good enough on that distribution?

small-language-model.body: The term is healthiest when it signals discipline. It is least healthy when it becomes yet another arms race, only with more smugness per parameter.
small-language-model.example: "The team finally adopted a small language model after spending six months paying frontier-model prices to classify support emails."
small-language-model.misuse: It is oversold in two opposite ways: as proof that frontier models are unnecessary for everything, or as if a compact model can casually replace them on hard reasoning and open-ended generation. Both claims are mostly branding with a benchmark attached.
small-language-model.note: Small is relative. The practical question is whether the model is small enough to change where and how it can be deployed. "Mini" in model branding usually signals this trade-off rather than a magical new class of intelligence.
small-language-model.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means cheaper inference, easier on-device or edge deployment, tighter control over predictable tasks, and a shorter list of things the system should be trusted to improvise.

translations.label: operations meaning
translations.text: A model sized for the workload rather than the keynote.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A more compact model that trades broad capability for lower serving cost and tighter deployment options.

small-language-model.whyExists: It exists because many business tasks are narrow, repetitive, and unforgiving about latency or cost. A smaller model can be the better engineering choice when the problem is constrained and the environment matters more than prestige.

askNext: What acceptance rate does the draft model achieve on real traffic?

speculative-decoding.body: The user sees a snappier model. The platform team sees one more elegant contraption that must justify its own existence.
speculative-decoding.example: "They introduced speculative decoding to cut latency, then discovered that fast token streaming is still not the same thing as a good answer."
speculative-decoding.misuse: It is sometimes described as free speed, as though the extra complexity carries no engineering cost or failure modes. In reality the benefit depends on workload shape, model pairing, and how often the bigger model rejects the draft.
speculative-decoding.note: This is one of the healthier AI terms because it points to a specific engineering method rather than a worldview.
speculative-decoding.practicalMeaning: In practice it means systems work: pairing models, tuning acceptance rates, measuring latency improvements honestly, and deciding whether the added serving complexity is worth the throughput gain.

translations.label: infrastructure meaning
translations.text: Spend a little complexity to buy back a noticeable amount of speed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality

speculative-decoding.whyExists: It exists because token-by-token generation is expensive and users do not enjoy watching a costly model think at the speed of ceremony. If a cheaper assistant can draft likely continuations, the main model can move faster through easy stretches.

askNext: What facts must survive compaction, and what evidence proves they were not lost?
Does the compacted state distinguish decisions from guesses?
Who reviews the summary when a long-running workflow starts behaving as though yesterday's mistake is policy?
state-compaction.body: State compaction is useful when it is treated as record-keeping, not poetry. The shorter version should earn its authority, preferably before the agent spends another hour obeying it.
state-compaction.example: "The coding agent improved once state compaction stopped keeping every failed experiment as if it were a constitutional precedent."
state-compaction.misuse: It gets treated as automatic understanding. A summary can preserve a task, but it can also flatten caveats, delete the one important exception, or make a false assumption look beautifully concise.
state-compaction.practicalMeaning: In practice it means deciding what an agent must remember, when to summarise it, how to attach evidence, and whether the compacted state can be audited before it becomes the next model's starting truth.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Shrink the working state without accidentally rewriting the job.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A summary that saves tokens, if it does not first save the wrong lesson.

state-compaction.whyExists: Long-running agents need continuity, but they cannot keep every message, tool result, and detour forever. Compaction exists to preserve the parts of state that still matter while discarding the operational compost.

askNext: What schema is being enforced, and who validates it?

structured-outputs.body: This is one of the most practically valuable patterns in modern AI systems because it reduces downstream chaos. It does not remove the need to evaluate the actual content.
structured-outputs.example: "The support triage improved once the model stopped composing heartfelt paragraphs and started returning severity, queue, confidence, and escalation reason in the schema."
structured-outputs.misuse: They get described as if a schema magically guarantees truth. It does not. Structured nonsense is still nonsense. A model can produce perfectly valid JSON containing invalid judgements, fabricated facts, or inappropriate actions.
structured-outputs.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema definitions, validation layers, retries, and careful prompt design so the model returns machine-usable data. It is often a major step up in reliability for extraction, classification, tool use, and workflow control.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A way to make model outputs less annoying for software to consume.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: More parseable responses, not inherently more correct ones.

structured-outputs.whyExists: Free-form language is nice for humans and annoying for software. Structured outputs exist because product workflows often need predictable fields, not prose with creative punctuation and a flair for near-miss compliance.

askNext: Do they mean Google's technical capability, its product rollouts, or Pichai's management style?
Is the claim about research strength, distribution power, or corporate caution?
Are we judging Google by frontier demos or by what it manages to ship at global scale?
sundar-pichai.body: When a firm already sits inside search, mobile, cloud, and office software, its AI posture is never just technical. It is managerial choreography with global defaults attached.
sundar-pichai.example: "The debate stopped being about model quality the moment someone said Sundar, and the room quietly shifted to distribution, regulation, and who gets to rewrite search without admitting it."
sundar-pichai.misuse: By treating him as either proof that Google has already won AI because it has reach, or proof that it has already lost because it once looked slow. Both stories flatten the more useful reality: incumbents move differently when they are defending search, ads, mobile platforms, cloud, and public trust all at once.
sundar-pichai.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Sundar usually means Google's AI strategy under executive discipline: Gemini folded through consumer and enterprise products, AI Overviews inserted into search, and a company trying to make aggressive technical change look like prudent continuity.

translations.label: boardroom meaning
translations.text: The AI transition as handled by a company with too much reach to behave like a startup.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for Google's AI strategy, risk tolerance, and product discipline.

sundar-pichai.whyExists: Because some public figures become shorthand for institutional posture. Invoking Sundar usually means discussing not just a person but Google's managerial style: cautious rollout, massive product surface area, and the awkward task of modernising the internet's default habits without wrecking the business that paid for them.

askNext: Is there real ecosystem lock-in?

super-app.body: AI is attractive to super app ambitions because conversation makes sprawl feel tidy. Whether the underlying product has actually become simpler is a separate question, and usually the more important one.
super-app.example: "The company said it was building a super app. Translation: it wanted to become the place where users stop leaving."
super-app.misuse: By calling any app with four tabs a super app. A true super app is not just feature-bloated. It is an ecosystem strategy.
super-app.practicalMeaning: Messaging, payments, commerce, support, booking, search, assistants, identity, and workflow all living under one roof, often with AI as the conversational front door.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A bid to own more of the user's time, transactions, and switching costs by collapsing multiple workflows into one controlled environment.

super-app.whyExists: Because platforms want distribution, retention, transaction volume, and data gravity. AI makes the pitch even more seductive by promising one interface for everything.

supply-chain-security.body: Supply-chain security belongs here because AI systems become organisational systems as soon as people rely on them. The useful version of the term points to ownership, review, escalation, or enforcement, not merely to a respectable phrase in a slide deck.
supply-chain-security.example: "The supply-chain security discussion began as process paperwork and ended as the only reason the agent was allowed anywhere near production."
supply-chain-security.misuse: It gets abused when a policy phrase is treated as if saying it creates the control. A label can name the discipline; it cannot perform the discipline for the organisation.
supply-chain-security.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means writing down who can act, who must approve, what gets logged, what can be reversed, and which failures become incidents rather than interesting anecdotes.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The part where permission, accountability, and review have to become real.

supply-chain-security.warningLabel: Governance vocabulary without enforcement is decorative risk management.
supply-chain-security.whyExists: It exists because the problems around Slopsquatting and Tool Poisoning are not only model problems. They are also ownership, permission, review, escalation, and accountability problems.

askNext: Which version of SWE-Bench are we talking about, and under what tooling assumptions?

swe-bench.misuse: By treating a strong SWE-Bench score as proof that a coding agent can replace engineering judgement, production review, or the small matter of understanding organisational context. It is a useful signal, not a complete hiring packet.
swe-bench.practicalMeaning: In practice it means measuring whether a system can navigate repo structure, identify relevant files, make coherent edits, and satisfy tests under benchmark conditions. It says much less about product sense, messy human requirements, or living with the resulting code.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A better benchmark than toy prompts, and still not the same thing as shipping software.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A constrained test of repository task performance that becomes less complete the moment it enters a sales deck.

swe-bench.whyExists: Because software engineering is harder to fake than toy code generation, and the field needed a public way to compare agents on real repositories, tests, and issue-driven fixes.

askNext: Did the model agree where it should have challenged?
Did it preserve the user's framing instead of testing it?
Would a more grounded answer have introduced uncertainty or correction?
sycophancy.body: This is one of the more important social failure modes in conversational AI because it can look like empathy, feel like support, and still make the user's judgement worse.
sycophancy.example: "The user wanted validation, not analysis, and the model was only too happy to provide both badly."
sycophancy.misuse: By using it to describe any polite response. True sycophancy is not just friendliness. It is miscalibrated agreement.
sycophancy.practicalMeaning: The model reinforces a user's bad diagnosis, faulty strategy, false certainty, or inflated self-assessment instead of correcting course.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model is preserving the user's preferred frame rather than helping them inspect whether that frame deserves to survive.

sycophancy.whyExists: Because chat systems are often trained to be helpful, pleasant, and non-confrontational. Under pressure, that can drift into obedience dressed as empathy.

askNext: Which real-world gaps is the synthetic data covering, and how will that coverage be validated?
What artefacts or biases might the generation process be introducing?
At what point does real usage data need to overrule the synthetic substitute?
synthetic-data.body: The synthetic version is often cleaner than life. That is precisely why one should worry.
synthetic-data.example: "Synthetic data got the project moving. The next problem was discovering which of its elegant assumptions reality had no intention of honouring."
synthetic-data.misuse: By treating it as a free replacement for reality. Useful synthetic data can extend a dataset. It cannot repeal the stubborn fact that models eventually meet actual users, actual documents, and actual mess.
synthetic-data.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bootstrapping scarce cases, filling scenario gaps, and trying to stretch data budgets without accidentally teaching the system a polished version of the wrong world.

translations.label: budget meaning
translations.text: A way to buy more examples without paying the full price of finding them in nature.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Manufactured data that can be valuable, provided it is judged against reality rather than mistaken for reality.

synthetic-data.whyExists: Because real data can be expensive, private, dangerous, incomplete, or slow to collect. Synthetic data offers a way to broaden coverage, test systems, and explore edge cases without waiting for the world to cooperate.

askNext: What real evidence informed these simulated users in the first place?
Are we using them to generate hypotheses or to avoid meeting actual customers?
Which decisions still require observation of real behaviour and incentives?
synthetic-user.body: The trouble with a simulated customer is that it will often tell you exactly what your process hoped to hear.
synthetic-user.example: "The synthetic users loved the onboarding because, unlike actual customers, they had been generated to find it coherent."
synthetic-user.misuse: It gets oversold as a replacement for actual user research. Synthetic users inherit the assumptions, biases, and blind spots of their prompts, source material, and model priors. They can imitate a segment. They cannot become one.
synthetic-user.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means using model-generated personas to pressure-test flows, draft hypotheses, or widen exploratory coverage before speaking to real people whose incentives, confusion, and refusal to behave neatly are the whole point.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A fast way to explore likely reactions before scheduling interviews or tests.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A synthetic proxy useful for hypothesis generation and dangerously tempting as a shortcut around actual users.

synthetic-user.warningLabel: Excellent for speed. Unsuitable as a substitute for reality.
synthetic-user.whyExists: It exists because recruiting real users is slow, messy, and expensive, while product teams are under constant pressure to validate ideas before lunch.

system-card.body: System card gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Claude Mythos Preview keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
system-card.example: "Once system card entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
system-card.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
system-card.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

system-card.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What instructions live in the system prompt, and which belong in code or policy instead?
How does the system behave when user input conflicts with hidden instructions?
What breaks if the system prompt is exposed, ignored, or partially obeyed?
system-prompt.body: Every serious AI product has one, whether the company talks about it or not. The interesting part is never merely that it exists. The interesting part is what else the team expects it to do.
system-prompt.example: "The app felt polished until the leaked system prompt revealed that half the brand voice was an anxious paragraph about never improvising legal advice."
system-prompt.misuse: By treating it as secret sauce, or worse, as a reliable security boundary. A system prompt is useful control text. It is not a magical wall separating trusted intent from hostile input.
system-prompt.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a standing instruction layer covering role, tone, refusal rules, output format, tool permissions, escalation behaviour, and the product's preferred version of reality.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The application's standing attempt to keep the model on script before users and retrieved text start arguing back.

system-prompt.warningLabel: An instruction layer, not a security model.
system-prompt.whyExists: Because most AI products need stable behaviour across many users and tasks. Someone has to tell the model what role it is playing, what rules matter, and how the chain of command is supposed to work.
